tool_call_sequence.description: Implements guideline-concordant first-line acute treatment (oxygen and triptan) with safety screening for contraindications and interactions before prescribing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis as cluster headache and links it to trigeminal autonomic features (autonomic symptoms such as ipsilateral lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, ptosis/miosis, facial sweating, or eyelid edema).

Elicits a brief targeted history supporting cluster headache (severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain; attack duration ~15–180 minutes; frequency up to multiple times/day; circadian/circannual pattern; restlessness/agitation) and notes family history as supportive but not required.

Screens for red flags and alternative diagnoses (e.g., new neurologic deficit, thunderclap onset, meningismus/fever, atypical prolonged duration, side-locked new headache, immunosuppression, age >50 new onset) and indicates need for urgent evaluation if present.

Explains treatment plan including both oxygen and a triptan as first-line acute options, with clear instructions on timing (at onset), administration method (non-rebreather oxygen; SQ triptan), and expected response.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (non-critical), checks for triptan contraindications (critical) including ischemic heart disease, prior stroke/TIA, peripheral vascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, hemiplegic/basilar migraine history, severe hepatic impairment, pregnancy considerations, and use of MAO inhibitors when relevant.

Performs required drug interaction checks before prescribing (critical), specifically avoiding concomitant ergot derivatives or another triptan within 24 hours; considers serotonergic agents and counsels about serotonin syndrome symptoms when clinically relevant; reviews current meds to ensure no high-risk interactions.

Provides counseling on adverse effects and precautions: oxygen safety (no smoking/open flames), triptan side effects (chest/neck tightness, paresthesias, flushing) and to seek urgent care for severe chest pain or neurologic symptoms.
Avoids inappropriate or unsafe recommendations (e.g., opioids for acute cluster headache; oral triptans as sole acute therapy; delayed treatment; benzodiazepines as primary therapy).

Documents or communicates a follow-up/monitoring intention (e.g., reassess response and attack frequency; consider preventive therapy such as verapamil and transitional therapy such as a short steroid bridge if frequent attacks), even if not required as an outcome tool for L2.
